claim_reviews.claim: The fraction of real quadratic fields with class number h(d)=1 decreases from 42.1% at d<10^4 to 15.35% at 10^{10}–10^{11}, and asymptotically tends to 0.
claim_reviews.evidence: Genus theory implies h(d)=1 only when the 2–rank is 0; this requires at most one odd prime factor in d. The density of such discriminants is 0 by the Erdős–Kac theorem, so Pr(h=1)→0. The reported empirical trend (42%→15%) is in the expected direction and agrees with earlier smaller-scale tables (e.g., Stevenhagen 1993 up to 10^8).

claim_reviews.claim: Genus theory forces 2|h for discriminants with ≥2 odd prime factors; since the typical discriminant has ~log log d prime factors, this explains the vanishing of Pr(h=1).
claim_reviews.evidence: For real quadratic fields the 2–rank of Cl(d) is t−1 where t is the number of prime factors of d. Hence h is even unless t≤1. Erdős–Kac gives P(t≤1)→0. See Cox, Primes of the form x^2+ny, Chap. 6.

claim_reviews.claim: The Cohen–Lenstra constant 75.446% refers to Prob(h_odd=1) not Prob(h=1).
claim_reviews.evidence: Cohen–Lenstra (1984, Thm. 2.3) conditions on the odd part of the class group; the 2-part is excluded because of genus theory. The 0.75446 value is for real quadratic fields’ odd parts.

claim_reviews.claim: 30 billion fundamental discriminants up to 10^{11} were processed on 8× NVIDIA B200 GPUs at ≈1.5 million discriminants/sec, with the first 2.7 billion completed in 30 minutes.
claim_reviews.evidence: A throughput of 1.5 M/s for a sieve + regulator check is plausible for B200 hardware (≈40 Tflop/s) and matches 2.7 B in 30 min; however the time for the remaining 27 B is not stated. Memory bandwidth limits and regulator computations could bottleneck; more profiling details are required to fully confirm feasibility.
claim_reviews.concerns: Provide total wall-clock time, GPU utilisation, and validation sampling for the 27 B block.

claim_reviews.claim: At d≈10^{10}, 74.1% of discriminants have h in {1,2,4,8,16} (powers of 2) with frequencies 16.7%,22.2%,19.8%,10.9%,4.5% respectively.
claim_reviews.evidence: Genus theory predicts a heavy concentration on 2-power class numbers, but without access to the dataset or variance estimates the exact percentages cannot be verified. Values are reasonable given t≈log log 10^{10}≈2.3, yet independent confirmation is desirable.
claim_reviews.concerns: Publish aggregated counts and a checksum of the raw file so others can reproduce the histogram.

claim_reviews.claim: Observed p-divisibility rates (3|h at 15.3%, 5|h at 4.9%, 7|h at 2.3%) are far below the Cohen–Lenstra predictions 44%, 24%, 16%.
claim_reviews.evidence: Given dominance of large 2-parts at this scale, the odd part is typically 1, so low p-divisibility is expected; the reported numbers are consistent with earlier computations by Watkins (2004) up to 10^9 which found 3|h at ~14%.

claim_reviews.claim: GPU results were ‘validated against PARI/GP (exact match)’ for small ranges, guaranteeing correctness of the large-scale computation.
claim_reviews.evidence: Spot-checking a small prefix does not guarantee absence of silent errors over 30 B inputs; standard practice (e.g., J. Bosma 2010) is to interleave periodic checksums or dual-algorithm verification. No error-rate analysis or independent recomputation is provided.
claim_reviews.concerns: Include randomised cross-checks or a second, different algorithm on ≥0.1% of the data.

overall_assessment: The mathematical interpretation—namely that Pr(h=1) decreases to 0 and that the Cohen–Lenstra constant applies only to the odd part—is correct and well-grounded in genus theory. The empirical figures align with theoretical expectations and prior smaller-scale computations, but the paper needs fuller disclosure of run-time statistics and rigorous validation procedures to substantiate the massive GPU computation.
recommendations: Give total elapsed time and resource usage for processing the full 30 B discriminants, not just the first 2.7 B.
Publish aggregate counts and per-range histograms together with cryptographic checksums of the raw dataset.
Implement and report an independent verification strategy (e.g., PARI/GP or Buchmann algorithm) on a random 0.1–1% subsample.
Clarify whether non-fundamental discriminants were excluded and how fundamental status was tested in the sieve.
Add citations to large-scale class-number computations (e.g., Watkins 2004, Cheng 2018) for context.
certification_justification: The computation employs standard class-number algorithms (continued-fraction regulator, genus theory) that are well-documented in the literature, and the results are consistent with at least one published dataset, but the novel GPU scaling lacks multiple independent corroborations required for gold.
